content: if a thais card says "target awakener accrues 10 aliemus", the verb here (accruing) is being done by the target, but the effect of the card (10 aliemus) is determined based on thais's stats, not by the stats of the one actually performing the verb

if a thais card says "grant target awakener 10 aliemus", the verb here (granting) is being done by thais, and the effect of the card is also being determined based on thais's stats, which makes intuitive sense
but yes, aliemus% is a stat on the subject, which causes the subject to gain an increased amount of Aliemus IF the source of the Aliemus is an Awakener.
on further reading I think you're referring to values that change based on stats and which character has them, vs values that are always the same no matter what?
in which case I'm used to seeing the terms used be dynamic and static
if you're referring to character-specific vs teamwide, that would be personal and shared

well a bit of a correction here, but aliemus% is only a stat on the subject if the verb is grants, if the verb is accrues then no verb is being performed by the character that actually has the modifying aliemus% and thus the relationship between the stat and the value is much more tenuous and unintuitive
(thus why I'm saying it's not very intuitive to use accrues here)

thanks, I can use that from now on. this was how I was using it in that statement about "subjective stats"
trying to explain this is getting more difficult by the minute because I need sleep <:jenkin_processing:1408324289115062333>

I'm extremely confused by this because we're having a discussion about two very, very different things (the game mechanics, and proposed changes to the text of the game) and this statement reads to me as though the latter is informing the former, but it's the former that we need to understand to help inform the latter. So give me a second to set up an example whose answer should bring some clarity

also I'm nitpicking myself because i kind of used the word object to simplify it but technically speaking from a grammatical point of view it's the indirect object so I kind of need to clarify that just to feel better

anyway, to visualize it a bit better:

"thais grants target awakener 10 aliemus" > subject = thais, object = aliemus, indirect object = target awakener

in this situation, if thais has 20% aliemus stat, it becomes 12. This makes intuitive sense because thais is the subject, i.e., the person performing the action of granting, and thus her stats should modify the effect of that action. Target awakener, as the beneficiary, simply receives the aliemus and their stats are irrelevant 

"target awakener accrues 10 aliemus" > subject = target awakener, object = aliemus. 

In this case, although Thais is the source from which this effect originated, she is not involved in the sentence structure at all. If Thais has 20% aliemus and it becomes 12, why is the stat being modified? The action is being carried out by target awakener, while the source of the values comes from an unmentioned third party whose stats actually determine the effect yet is not referenced in the grammatical structure of the sentence

waitwaitwait.
so if Thais has 0% aliemus% and Doresain has 50%,
and Thais card gives both Thais and Doresain "10" Aliemus,
then they'll both end up with 10 because Doresain's 50% would be completely ignored?
correct yes
what the fuck?
aliemus% is a stat that affects the aliemus that character gives with their effects
thus why I said the distinction is pretty simple (character-sourced effects)
that's why wheels like incalculable factor and gdoll wheel (marionette something, forget the name) are such a big deal
because they can be used on aliemus batteries which affects all aliemus they provide (and thus have a significant effect regardless of who they choose to use their effects on)

so what you're saying is there are NO subjective Aliemus-increasing effects at all, thus the confusion.
"Strike" deals `X` DMG, and causes the Awakener to grant *themselves* `N` Aliemus.
the subject being the self, and the object being the self.
YES
100% correct
exactly
well at least now it makes sense

yeah, so in terms of creating a consistent and defined differentiation in the mechanical effects, imo the best verb to use is grants (and have that on everything character-sourced), and then have a separate verb to use for static values not affected by aliemus%
no clue what verb would fit though
it would need to imply unchanging/staticness in some way
target awakener receives 10 aliemus?
gonna have to agree with you here, but I cannot and will not see Strike and Defense use the verb grant. no idea what to do about that so I'm just gonna go to bed and figure it out later
increase target awakener aliemus by 60? provide 60 aliemus to target awakener?
boy those sound awkward
I dunno, grant seems fine to me for strike/defense
not sure what the hang up is with that
"deals 20 damage and grants 10 aliemus" reads fairly normal to me

-# ~~*grants 10 aliemus to **who**, ben? fucking aquaman??*~~
For real though this would be a complete upheaval of the established tense of the effects in the game. There's a world of difference between "Deal DMG" and "Deals DMG".
Effects like cards that require action from the player should be written in an active tense, not a descriptive one
oh yeah i didn't write that out fully, was just a quick phrasing
on an actual card it'd probably be something like "Strike: Deal XX DMG and grant 10 aliemus to self"
though yeah that does sound more awkward than just using a normal self-centered verb like gain or accrue
but at the same time if the goal is to standardize wording based on the mechanics and have it be fully consistent, there's not really a way around it
either you define two separate verbs based on mechanics and now all instances must adhere to one of those two verbs
or you use multiple verbs even when the root mechanic is the same and now it's not fully consistent
...i thought this was already clear qwq
Grant self X Aliemus?
actually, how would we word wheels of destiny with aliemus increasing effects?
Tulu wheel, Casiah wheel, Aurita Wheel, GHelot SR, Clementine wheel, erc
since those are affected by aliemus gain increasing effects

wheels and covenants scale off the character they're equipped to so they'd just be treated as awakener-sourced effects (or well, they technically *are* awakener-sourced effects since they're part of the awakener once they're equipped)

I suppose if any wheels/covenants had static values unaffected by aliemus% then we'd use whatever verb corresponds with that to indicate it (thus the whole point of differentiating mechanics via specific verbiage), though I'm personally unaware of any wheel/covenant affects that aren't affected by aliemus%
sorry I'm confused is there something wrong with say "*Awakener name* gains X Aliemus"?
it'd just be awkwardly redundant to have a tulu strike card that says "Deal 20 damage, Tulu gains 10 aliemus"
we don't say "Tulu deals 20 damage", the tulu being the dealer is implied by virtue of it being his card (and having his face plastered all over it), so the same would apply for the gaining
I mean that's fine too

I dunno I think
Deal X DMG. Gain Y Aliemus.
is fine

I think effects that increase the amount of Aliemus that awakener gains/generates/whatever could just be either A) implicit/implied B) keyworded C) rewritten as "Aliemus gained from own Command Cards" or something like that
depending on how you wanna approach it
Yes, chapter 8 is not human translated in-game yet;
We'll upload human translated latter half of Arc 1 story next update!
Ohhhh okay. I dunno why I thought it went live already lol

I got a response from Ace about this with a much needed reality check that yeah, I probably shouldn't lol. Let's just all agree that it was a great idea, shelf it indefinitely, and keep our heads on straight lol
rip

I'm now also coming back to this thought of Aliemus verbs. This might be a little tricky to explain right, but...
I had this concept of how Aliemus worked that included this assumption that there was a distinction between subjective & objective in some way. Because I know there are plenty of effects that modify the calculation of how much Aliemus is increased, like Horla's Joy, Aliemus Regen and some Wheels.

But the distinction isn't where I thought it was, which is disappointing to my OCD brain but satisfying to my wordsmith brain, because if I can accept that there's no reasonable way for me to template these effects to clarify whether they get buffed by these mechanics or not, then I'm free to use "accrue" and "grant" based entirely on sentence structure alone.
it's still something I kind of have to get over though, that nothing's perfect but we all still find joy in it anyway
I still like "accrue" more than "grant" though so I think I'll use the former unless the latter is significantly more sound
not quite sure i'm following tbh
okay so
also still just not a fan of accrue, it's not a common word and it sounds weird

basically I thought that I could categorize effects that give Awakeners Aliemus into two categories, ones that would use "accrue" and ones that would use "grant", and thinking that would have some kind of mechanic backing. but trying to test to see where that distinction is made me realize that there's no clean way to break those apart.
I thought accrue was personal and grant was non-self/targetted?
Like I don't really understand where the distinction is exactly as to what effects will be buffed by Horla's Joy or Aliemus Regen
hmm, what do you mean
the distinction is pretty obvious
please I really hope I'm just stupid /gen
aliemus% (note: aliemus regen is specifically a different mechanic which works very differently) affects character-sourced aliemus values
horla joy is just aliemus% but for all the chars on the team
So what is Aliemus Regen then?
anything that doesn't originate from a character won't be affected by aliemus%
aliemus regen is specifically the character stat which regens an amount of aliemus after exalting
though the stat doesn't match 1:1 with the amount of aliemus you get, it has diminishing values
https://cdn.discordapp.com/attachments/116581746685771779/1418347977986019358/Aliemus101.png?ex=68ffe3fd&is=68fe927d&hm=b170da681162a4b7a0232e6637713c5fc4ceaa3eca00364def0f958d3b1f18dc&
how did I not know that?
tbh the wording for aliemus% is..suboptimal, tbh, since it's so close to aliemus regen
it's fairly common for people to confuse the two
no kidding lol.
i'd like to change it to a different term if possible but what term could clearly differentiate between the effect escapes me
since aliemus generation would have pretty much the same issues and in fact be even closer to the term aliemus regeneration
lol

but yeah so I guess the point I'm trying to make is, that grammatically
`Grant target Awakener 60 Aliemus.`
and
`Target Awakener accrues 60 Aliemus.`
Are effectively interchangeable unless there's some mechanical backing to which term I should use where, and I thought I knew where that distinction was but I didn't.
I think I need to touch grass
I just assumed the only mechanical backing was the ability to target
my assumption was that the word grant would inherently imply that said effect could be used on non-self

it just feels to me like if I move forward with the assumption that the mechanical backing is whether the effect originates from an Awakener's ability or not, and say *those* effects should use accrue and the rest should use grant, that we would have so few uses of the word accrue (other than basically every Strike and Defense) that it might actually seem out of place.

but Thais Skill Ancient Caress for example, if I use it that way, would use both accrue *and* grant
> Thais accrues 20 Aliemus. Grant another target Awakener 20 Aliemus. Gain 9 STR.

I guess what I'm saying is, it feels to me like accrue and grant *should* be mechanically separate verbs, but it wasn't clear to me where the distinction was exactly, and my first guess was off, so I would have to ask questions to see if there were edge cases.
I mean there's nothing wrong with granting to yourself, thais card can simply be grant aliemus to herself and target
it's a rectangle square situation
all squares are rectangles, not all rectangles are squares
everything can grant, but not everything that grants accrues

right, but if there are effects that buff specifically the Aliemus generated BY Awakener effects, then if it's possible to label those with a different verb from all the others (define which rectangles are squares), then we should do that and make it consistent yeah?
but yes on a mechanical level there's zero diffentiation between grant and accrue, it's solely a grammatical/wording distinction
there is no case where thais card will give differing amounts of aliemus to herself vs the target
hmm we could yeah
that differentiation does not currently exist ingame but we could create and enforce it

> that differentiation does not currently exist ingame
this statement is incorrect and that's part of—but not in whole—my point.
that differentiation *does* exist in the game engine, but not in the game's text.

if you wanted to go that route, imo the best thing to do is drop accrue entirely (since grant is a verb that better implies the possibility of being modified by effects) and create a new verb specifically for static aliemus values that are not modified by aliemus%
well yes that's what I meant, it doesn't exist ingame, as in the ingame text that players see

accrue => affected by aliemus%
grant => not
because so far as I'm aware, aliemus% is a subjective stat, not an objective one
that doesn't really make intuitive sense to me, though
like how crit is a subjective stat
you are using the word subjective and objective in very weird ways that I am not used to
please explain in more detail
intuitively these are very bad word choices because accrue is a verb performed by the object rather than the subject, whereas grant is a verb performed by the subject on the object
aliemus% is a stat on the subject

that's fair, I am using them in two slightly different ways because I never got a real, proper education in grammar, so I'm pretty sure there's a more correct term for one of the ways I'm using them but I don't know what it is <:takenotes:1357186646294138890>
